questionText: Find the coordinates of the turning point of the curve $y = x^2 - 6x + 11$.

steps.explanation: Completing the square rewrites the quadratic so that the turning point can be read off directly.
Half of $-6$ is $-3$, which will appear inside the squared bracket.

steps.description: Complete the square on the x terms.
steps.explanation: Expanding $(x-3)^2$ gives $x^2 - 6x + 9$, so we subtract $9$ to compensate.
Replacing $x^2 - 6x$ and keeping the $+11$ gives an equivalent expression.
Combining $-9 + 11$ gives $+2$, so the curve is $(x-3)^2 + 2$.
In this form the turning point is at $(h, k)$, because the square is smallest (zero) when $x = h$.

steps.description: Identify the type of turning point.
steps.explanation: Since the $x^2$ coefficient is positive, the parabola opens upward and the turning point is a minimum.
The turning point is the minimum point $(3, 2)$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $y = x^2 - 6x + 11 = (x-3)^2 + 2$, so the minimum turning point is $(3, 2)$.
